speaker: interviewee
text: Number 15 with Michael Jackson. And he was talking about his cousin who was mistreated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was captured by the Russian with-- with other Hungarian Jewish labor units. And they were mistreated. And he died already. And never-- he never came home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We are talking now-- we are in-- in May 1944. The-- I was telling that we thought that we-- we were surrendered. Of course, the [NON-ENGLISH] the [NON-ENGLISH] is called the fence. This-- the-- the soldiers, the Hungarian soldiers that were watching us in Hungary, they are called the [NON-ENGLISH]. They were 14, 15 soldiers, a sergeant, and the commander. The commander was usually an officer. It could be a captain. Or it could be a major or it could be lieutenant. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They were surrendered-- surrounded. And we-- we saw-- we saw somebody-- very-- very pleasant things, we could-- I should say, we saw. The Germans were mostly mechanized more than the Hungarian army. The Hungarians had horses and wagons. And they-- they had-- they were having truck. The had also-- the Hungarians also had trucks, but not as many as the Germans. And when they were moving back, the Germans were running so bad, that they-- they-- they choked up the-- the highways. So they were even throwing the Hungarians off the highways in order so that-- that-- they could pass through there. So there was-- we saw a conflict between the two-- between the two. And the Hungarians-- of course, the Hungarians don't understand German. And the German don't understand Hungarian. They were cursing at each other. But we-- we-- we understood both languages. But somehow or other, we got-- we got through there. They broke-- the-- the Germans broke out. And-- and-- and came back all the way to a city called Miedzyrzec Podlaski. That is 50 miles-- 50 miles from Warsaw, between Warsaw and Brest Litovsk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stopped there. And also we worked there, loading, unloading, digging, and-- and what not. The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At-- at that place, in Miedzyrzec Podlaski, I found out after the war that this was a-- a 90% Jewish city. There were 20,000 people in that city. And there was 18,000 Jews. It was entirely a Jewish city, with-- with-- with a Jewish police and fire brigades and everything. We didn't know-- we didn't know that when we were there. In that city, the Germans had a-- a-- they had some booths, steel booths. And that they would-- they would wash horses in there, would steam them with some kind-- horses that were infected with something. We also had horses. And they also had automatic-- those machines that would wash military blankets and whatnot. And, of course, they were all infected with all kinds of-- of diseases. And so they-- they used the Jews to do this-- this dirty work for them. Of course, we were dumb. At that time, we didn't know that we had to push-- those horses had all kinds of sores on-- on-- on there. And we had to push in these-- in these-- all-- all-- they would lock them up. All you would-- they would have out is the head and then neck. And then when washing those-- those blankets and clothing and folding it, and-- and-- and this was full of disease. I-- I worked there a few days. And one day, I came-- I came from-- from work-- and-- and we stayed in empty houses. One day, I came and I-- and I just collapsed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I had-- I-- I-- I had an infection of the tudogyulladas in Hungarian. This is a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] an infection of the lung, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not TB. The-- the-- When somebody gets a-- a-- pneumonia. I caught pneumonia. This was with high fever. I collapsed. And I don't know if I mentioned that before, that each-- each of these labor units, Jewish labor units, had either one or two and sometimes three Jewish physicians. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In our unit, we had three. One got sick a lot before that. And he-- he was sent away. I never know what happened to him. But the one-- the one physician was from Sighet. And that's where my-- my uncle was a teacher. And he knew him. We were friendly with him. His name was Ecker, Dr. Ecker. Well, they didn't do the physical work. They were-- they didn't do-- they were carrying red-- Red Cross bands. And they were carrying-- they would have, like, first aid. If you cut yourself, or-- they had bandages. They would-- that's about all that they could do. But if somebody was sick-- they-- they called-- when I collapsed, they called the doctor. And he examined me. He listen to the chest. And he said, you have-- you have pneumonia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he was not allowed, this doctor, Jewish doctor, was not allowed to-- to give somebody off from work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As long as he-- if he had 40-- 40 temperature, which is, I think, 102, or 103, 40, 41-- but he realized that I-- I-- I'm unable to go to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took me the next morning when we were-- had to march out, he took me in this city, Miedzyrzec Podalski. There was a Hungarian battalion was there. So he took me to that commander. And they had-- they had doctors there. And he took me over there. And he reported that Michael [? Jakubowics-- ?] [? Mihaly ?] they called me in Hungarian-- that he has a tudogyualladas, a-- a-- a pneumonia. And he-- and this doctor, Hungarian doctor, asked him, how high is his temperature? And he told him, he's 40 or 41. He says, give him 24 hours and then bring him back to me. So I got-- I got 24 hours off. So we were sleeping, of course, on-- on the floor. I don't remember how or where, we did get some straw from somewhere to-- to spread out. The 24 hours passed. But I still had fever. And he took me again. He gave me another 24 hours. After 48 hours or-- or-- or three, four days later, the temperature went down. He took me back to this-- to this doctor, and he, again, asked, does he have fever? He says, no, he doesn't have fever, but he is very weak. He says, if he doesn't-- this is the-- if he doesn't have fever, he-- he has to go out to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: This Dr. Ecker, he realized that I-- I won't be able to-- I was so weak from-- from this-- this fever a few days, so he went to our sergeant from-- from our [NON-ENGLISH]. And he told them, I can't hold this man here, because his fever is done, but he cannot go out to work. You have to do something for him. And he was-- this-- this sergeant, his name was Szacserovszki. The reason why I remember his name, he was a very-- a very decent guy. It's a matter of fact, a few months later, he deserted to the partisans, because we saw him later. He-- he disappeared from us altogether.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, OK, I'm going to keep him here in the kitchen to-- to-- to peel-- to peel potatoes, to help in the kitchen, because we have-- our group had a kitchen, a field kitchen, you know that was-- that were-- they-- they were cooking whatever-- whatever the rations, the rations, probably, [? and ?] we were getting.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the boys in the kitchen, they knew-- those were all our boys-- they knew how sick I was. And they just let me-- let me sit there and stay and-- and do-- do nothing. A few days later, this doctor, again, asked this sergeant to-- in order for me to recuperate from that-- from that tudogyulladas, to get a little bit more rest, to assign me our [NON-ENGLISH]-- our group had six horses, three wagons, six horses. To the-- to the-- to-- I got an assignment to-- to take care of the horses. And during the day, we didn't go anywhere. So the horses-- this was in the summer-- to take the horses out, out on a-- on a, you know, on the pasture. So after a-- after a couple of weeks that I went through, I got back-- back to my-- my strength. And-- and I was back-- it was back into the-- into-- into the regular group. Then from there-- from there we were sent to Wlochy. Wlochy was on an outskirt-- outskirt of the city of Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Russians, by that time, this was in May. And I might be wrong here with the dates. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The-- the Polish uprising started about that time. And we-- we were at the outside of the city. The city was burning. And the Russians were first, crossed the Vistula river around, around the city of Warsaw. But we didn't know it then. We know it now what happened that Stalin ordered-- because the uprising of the city Warsaw was under the tutelage of the Polish government in exile in London, and they didn't want that government to be successful. Because it was the London government, it would be more allied with the West. So they-- they pulled their forces back from across the Vistula River. The Vistula divides the city of-- of Warsaw. And they let the city burn to a crisp. And-- and-- and they wouldn't give them any help. We were on this side. And we were watching the city-- the city burn-- burn down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's-- it's-- and I found out months later that the-- the Warsaw ghetto was burned-- was burned down in 1943. But we didn't know about it. We were so close. We didn't know about it. And I remember sitting close by the city in Wlochy. And there was an old Pole sitting on-- on a bench and watching, seeing that the city is on fire. And-- and at night time, it-- it was awful, awful sight to see a city going up in fire. The Germans had a special group, Ukrainians, trained. And they went-- surrounded the city. And they went from block to block, whatever they could take out from the housing. And they were just-- just burning the city down. It was-- you could see children, small children, with white flags, running alone, running out, outside trying to run out of the city. It was a terrible, terrible sight. And I remember this Pole-- Pole, he was saying-- he didn't know who we were. He-- he was talking to me in Polish. And he was crying. He says, this is already the 17 day that Warsaw is burning. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From there-- from there, we-- we worked around-- around the Vistula River in Modlin a month or-- or so. It was supposed to be-- supposed to be put up-- put up a line of defense that the Germans and Hungarians would hold back. The Russians were pushing. By-- by October, November, we were all the way down to the-- almost the city of Krakow. And-- what was that-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Over there, the-- the-- the Vistula River-- in Germany call it the Weichsel-- the Vistula River over there divides and there is a little island that is a few kilometers long. And it-- it became that the Hungarians were supposed to hold this part of the Vistula River, because the Russians were on the other side. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the way the Russians were on that side of that side of the river, it was more like-- like they were on a-- on a hill. And this side was in the valley.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we couldn't go there working, digging trenches on that island. You couldn't go there during the day, because they could see us. They would shoot across the-- across the-- the river. So we would work at night. And we were stationed about five kilometers away from the river. And we would go out-- this was already-- this was already in-- in in October, November-- we were going out in the evening when it-- it's dark. It took us an hour to get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they had some little-- little bridges that they made and-- and boats to get to that island. It was very easy to dig in that, because it was just purely sand. But it was-- when it was wet and raining in-- in October, November time, you were going in the water. And there was nowhere where you could sit down. It was terrible. At that place, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were also so disgusted with that work that we didn't-- we were going-- they-- they ordered us to go in a single line because it's safer to go on a single line than on-- in three abreast or four abreast that normally units go. But you could hear the-- the-- the swishes of the-- of the bullets. And for some reason, we didn't-- we didn't we didn't fear. We had no fear because it-- it was such miserable life that we didn't-- we didn't fear anymore-- anymore whatever happened. Anyway we worked there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till sometimes-- till sometimes in December. And then in December-- there-- yeah, I-- I have to-- I have to mention this here. From our group, there was one boy that was from Romania. He spoke Romanian. In one of the [NON-ENGLISH], one of the-- of the Hungarian soldiers-- he was a-- from Romania. It was a mixture somehow. He was all-- all there. And they-- they were able to speak Romanian. They talked together that they would desert. But you couldn't swim that river over because it's a huge river. But this soldier, he was able to get somehow a little boat. And that boy, one of the Jewish boys-- his name was Ganz-- they got together. And they, by boat, crossed over the-- the Vistula River. And they-- they surrendered to the Russians. Next morning when we were getting to become-- we-- we were terribly scared that the guy-- we didn't know what happened. That-- here was a Jewish boy missing. And he one-- one of the-- of the-- of the soldiers missing. But next day, the Russians, through loudspeakers, announce to us, yesterday two of your people came over. We warn you. You should surrender. You should come over. If not, you are going to have the same. And then, you know, they would start shooting and cursing in Russian. And they would also play Hungarian-- Hungarian melodies. You could hear it across with loud speakers. that's how close we were. There were day, we were loaded up-- brought in, and loaded up to the-- on the trains. We heard that in this city there were some Jews working in a factory, but we couldn't-- we couldn't-- we couldn't get in touch with them whatsoever. And I think if you-- if you heard of I think that was one of the factories that was in that-- in that city that he-- he was there. We heard about it when we were there, that there are some people that are- are still working here, Jewish people in a factory. They loaded us up. And it was very, very strange that all this time we were never-- never accommodated with-- with transportation. We had to carry everything. We had to walk. But that time, they were-- we had four [NON-ENGLISH], four groups. We were roughly about, I would say, 800 people, because a labor unit in the military was 220 people, besides the 15 soldiers. But many of them fell by the wayside that they got sick. And they were taken away. We never heard from them what happened to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So but I figured if four-- four of those units were loaded up on a train. And we were surprised that all of the sudden they're loading us on trains-- where are-- where are we going?

speaker: interviewee
text: But after a day or so-- the trains were going very slow at that time. After a day or two, the train would go-- would go a few hours and then would stop and stay for hours and hours. I don't know what, either the-- the rails were bombarded and what was the trouble. But one-- once we came to a city, and then all of sudden we saw this is the set of Breslau. This is a German, industrial city. Then we realized that we are being shipped into Germany. In Breslau, the city was-- was bombed. But where we could still see a lot of-- a lot of chimneys, we could see, were still billowing with smoke. And by the-- by the 23rd of December 1944, we arrived in Flossenburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have three minutes. Do you-- you want me to read the-- our arrival impression of the-- of the first-- no? So we'll just-- we'll just-- I'll synopsis what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Flossenburg early in the morning. The Hungarian soldiers-- the Hungarian soldiers unloaded us and put us out in-- in-- in threes, the way we normally would go. I was always in the front-- in the front of the-- of the group. And we started walking. They were with-- with weapons. They probably knew where they are sending us. But we didn't. We got into the city. This was the 23rd of December. It was-- it was very cold. But it was-- it was dry, not-- not snowing or anything. The city-- we-- we crossed the city. The city was very big, big traffic in the city. We could see a lot of young soldiers on-- on furlough for the holidays with-- with their girlfriends walking in a group. They-- they-- they were giving us the dirty looks. And as a matter of fact, we-- we could hear some-- some very nasty words, [SPEAKING GERMAN], that the-- the people from the sidewalks would-- would comment on us. Then all of a sudden, SS appears, German-- German SS, they appeared from somewhere. And they took over the command. But they didn't like the way we were in threes. So they-- they started rearranging us. And-- and instead of three abreast, they wanted us four abreast. In meantime, everybody had-- was getting clubbed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the camp was on a hill. And we crossed the entire village. They saw us We crossed there. And we were climbing up to this hill. And from far away, I could see a big sign. It said in German, Kriegsgefangenenlager. So I thought to myself, Kriegsgefangenenlager means war prisoners. So I thought to myself, if they-- if they--
